article_title: Early and Late Reoperation Rates With Various MIS Techniques for Adult Spinal Deformity Correction
label: Figure 1.
caption: Preoperative and postoperative lateral radiographs of a patient from the HYB group who underwent T9-S1/pelvis reconstruction and incurred PJK.

article_title: Early and Late Reoperation Rates With Various MIS Techniques for Adult Spinal Deformity Correction
label: Figure 2.
caption: Preoperative and postoperative PA and lateral radiographs of a patient corrected with cMIS and demonstrating lucency of the S1 screws and possible pseudarthrosis at L5-S1.

article_title: Negative Sagittal Balance Following Adult Spinal Deformity Surgery
label: Figure 1.
caption: Representative standing, lateral radiographs taken preoperatively (top row) and postoperatively (bottom row). (A) Patient in group 1 (negative), with a postoperative sagittal vertical axis (SVA) <−10 mm. (B) Patient in group 2 (neutral), with a postoperative SVA >−10 mm and <+10 mm. (C) Patient in group 3 (positive), with a postoperative SVA >+10 mm.

article_title: Quantitative Assessment of Celiac and Superior Mesenteric Artery Diameters in Adult Spinal Deformity Surgery Using Three-dimensional Computed Tomography
label: Figure 1.
caption: Three-dimensional images of enhanced computed tomography of the celiac artery (CA), superior mesenteric artery, and aorta (A). Reconstructed long-axis view of enhanced computed tomography of the CA (B). Short-axis view of the CA (C, D). Diameter of the stenotic part (a) and distal normal part (b).

article_title: Quantitative Assessment of Celiac and Superior Mesenteric Artery Diameters in Adult Spinal Deformity Surgery Using Three-dimensional Computed Tomography
label: Figure 2.
caption: Reconstructed sagittal (A, C) and axial (B, D) enhanced computed tomography images showing the median arcuate ligament (MAL) and the celiac artery. The MAL exists superior to the celiac trunk and does not overlap (A, B, normal). The MAL exists inferior to the celiac trunk and overlaps the proximal portion (C, D, MAL overlap).

article_title: Quantitative Assessment of Celiac and Superior Mesenteric Artery Diameters in Adult Spinal Deformity Surgery Using Three-dimensional Computed Tomography
label: Figure 3.
caption: Sagittal reconstructed enhanced computed tomography images showing the MAL and CA levels (line circles). The distance between the MAL and CA (DMC) was defined as the differences in levels between the MAL and CA (two dotted lines).

article_title: Quantitative Assessment of Celiac and Superior Mesenteric Artery Diameters in Adult Spinal Deformity Surgery Using Three-dimensional Computed Tomography
label: Figure 4.
caption: Sagittal reconstructed enhanced computed tomography images of the MAL and vertebra. The distance between the MAL and a vertebra (DMV) was defined as the shortest distance between the MAL and the anterior edge of the vertebra (black arrow).

article_title: Quantitative Assessment of Celiac and Superior Mesenteric Artery Diameters in Adult Spinal Deformity Surgery Using Three-dimensional Computed Tomography
label: Figure 5.
caption: Preoperative (A) and postoperative (B) radiographs. Preoperative (C) and postoperative (D) sagittal reconstructed enhanced computed tomography images. The distance between the MAL and the vertebra (DMV, black arrow) was shortened, and CA stenosis was observed after surgery (D).

article_title: Quantitative Assessment of Celiac and Superior Mesenteric Artery Diameters in Adult Spinal Deformity Surgery Using Three-dimensional Computed Tomography
label: Figure 6.
caption: Preoperative (A) and postoperative (B) illustrations of the distance between the MAL and vertebra (DMV, black dotted arrow). Reduced DMV due to thoracolumbar kyphosis correction causes the MAL compression on the CA. Figure 6 has never been published in previous reports.

article_title: Minimally Invasive Robotic-Assisted Complex Adult Spinal Deformity Correction in a Surgical Specialty Hospital: Bringing Adult Spinal Deformity Care Closer to Home
label: Figure 1
caption: (a) Preoperative images of a patient with sagittal malalignment; (b) postoperative images showing expandable LLIF cages at L1–4, 3D-printed ALIF spacers at L4–S1 and robot-assisted posterior fixation from T10 to pelvis.
